Name: pac_bayes_quantization
Title: PAC-Bayes Bounds for Mixed-Precision Quantization in Low-Latency Machine Learning Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that PAC-Bayes bounds can be used to derive strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference in mixed-precision quantization, ensuring a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, EMQ, and AMP-ViT have explored mixed-precision quantization for efficient machine learning inference. However, these methods lack rigorous guarantees on latency and accuracy. Our proposal distinguishes from existing literature by incorporating PAC-Bayes bounds to provide a theoretical framework for mixed-precision quantization, ensuring a balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel approach to mixed-precision quantization for low-latency machine learning inference, leveraging PAC-Bayes bounds to derive strictly bounded-overhead protocols. Our framework ensures a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, providing rigorous guarantees on latency and accuracy. We propose to use spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable protocols for latency-critical inference, minimizing hidden-state perturbation and enforcing strict wall-clock budgets. Experimental evaluations will be conducted to validate the proposed approach, using first-class latency benchmarks with explicit perturbation-cost and memory-bandwidth accounting.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed PAC-Bayes bounds for mixed-precision quantization on benchmark datasets such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10.
Compare the performance of our approach with state-of-the-art mixed-precision quantization methods such as SNIP, EMQ, and AMP-ViT.
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the effect of different hyperparameters on the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may require significant computational resources for deriving PAC-Bayes bounds and spectral stability bounds.
The accuracy of the proposed approach may be sensitive to the choice of hyperparameters and the quality of the training data.
